old_claim.note: Mercantilism Economic Theory is contextual infrastructure or shared knowledge, not a strict hard prerequisite.

new_claim.note: Grotius's early sea-law work was commissioned around the VOC's seizure of a Portuguese merchant ship, making joint-stock overseas companies a concrete commercial context for the legal argument.

source_shape.source_locator: Life and works section says De iure praedae was apparently commissioned by the V.O.C. around 1603 to defend the capture of a Portuguese merchant ship by a V.O.C. fleet near Singapore.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source ties Grotius's early sea-law argument to a concrete VOC commission over a captured Portuguese merchant ship.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Joint-stock overseas companies capture the documented institutional context better than mercantilism, which is a broad economic theory not directly named by the source.

ontology_before: The Grotius node depended on broad mercantilist economic theory, laundering a general economic ideology into a specific legal-history claim.
ontology_after: The Grotius node depends on joint_stock_companies as the concrete VOC-style commercial institution behind the early sea-law controversy.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: international_law_early_grotius now depends on joint_stock_companies and no longer directly depends on mercantilism_economic_theory.
would_reject_if: The node is narrowed to De Jure Belli ac Pacis only and excludes Mare Liberum or De iure praedae context.
A source shows mercantilist theory was the direct intellectual prerequisite rather than VOC commercial litigation and sea-law controversy.
The joint_stock_companies node is narrowed away from chartered overseas trading companies.
short_reason: The VOC/joint-stock company context is sourced directly; mercantilism was too broad for the corrected scope.
